documents.source: Vera Karelina, Russian woman labor activist and revolutionary, describing her work as a political organizer among factory workers in St. Petersburg in the 1890s, memoir published in the Soviet Union in 1926
documents.content:
“I began the mass organization of women in the Factory Workers’ Society. Each branch of the
Society elected its own chairwoman and secretary. The chairwoman of one of the branches was
Maria Soldatova, who worked at a cigarette factory.
Maria was forty years old and illiterate. But she possessed inexhaustible energy and natural
organizational ability and could understand and think quickly. Despite her illiteracy, Maria was
unanimously elected chairwoman of her branch. She often complained about her illiteracy,
and would always say, ‘If only I could read and write, I could do so much more for the factory
and the Society.’ When someone offered to teach her to read and write, she began to study
diligently.”

documents.source: Ottoman postcard showing women and girls supervised by a male manager (the standing figure in the background) at a silk factory in the Ottoman city of Bursa, 1902 Chronicle / Alamy Stock Photo
He-Yin Zhen, Chinese woman political activist, “What Women Should Know About Communism,” essay published in a Chinese language journal in 1907, while she was living as a political exile in Japan
documents.content:
“Everywhere in Shanghai there are silk factories, cotton mills, weaving factories, and laundries,
employing thousands of women. These women work all day into the evening.
Women suffer just to get hold of our meager bowl of rice. My fellow women: why don’t you
have any money to buy food? It is because the rich have stolen our property. They have forced
the majority of the people into poverty and starvation. Look at the wives and daughters of the
elite. They live luxuriously with plenty of food.
Society has accepted this state of affairs because, people say, as long as a woman has a job she
would not fear starvation.... So the families that are very poor are sending their daughters and
daughters-in-law to work in factories. But factories are built by investors to make a profit and, if
you work in a factory, you remain at the mercy of its capitalist owners.”

documents.source: Report by the provincial governorate of the Vladimir District of the Russian Empire on the occupations of the rural population of the district, published as part of a comprehensive government survey of the occupational patterns of the Russian population, 1912
documents.content:
“According to a local census taken in 1897, 52.2% of the male population of the Vladimir District
were involved in some type of manufacturing occupation rather than in farming. The fact that
such a significant portion of men in the villages are working in non-farming jobs means that
often it is the women in these households that must do the majority of farm field work, which
has traditionally been a male task.
In these circumstances, in many areas of the district, women take the leading roles in the
households. Not only do women plow, plant, rake, and gather the hay and grain, but they often
execute the social obligations of men. Many women now serve as village council leaders or
members, and even as village tax collectors, positions that were all previously reserved for
men.”

documents.source: Toshio Takai, Japanese woman labor activist, memoir describing events in the 1910s and 1920s, first published in 1980
documents.content:
“I was around twelve years old in 1913 when a recruiter for female textile workers came to
our village. He stopped by our house and said, ‘How about it? Wouldn’t you like to send your
daughter to work for the Ogaki Company? We have dormitories, the work is easy and pleasant.
There’s silk spinning and you get paid thirteen sen [0.13 yen] per day. We feed you and clothe
you, so it’s really a help to your parents.’ Once I heard all this, I made up my mind. I reckoned
that with that kind of money I could buy my mother and sister something, so I said, ‘I will do it.
I’ll work.’
What we had heard was paradise; the reality turned out to be hell. The thirteen sen we were
supposed to earn per day was before deductions were made for the food we ate and the soap,
toilet paper, and straw sandals we used, so in the end we were left with less than one sen per
day.
My job was to clean up waste pieces of silk thread from the factory floor. For twelve hours a day
I was standing or walking among the machines. My legs became stiff and my feet were always
swollen.”

documents.source: Johanna Cornelius, White South African labor activist, autobiographical statement prepared for press release when she was elected as a National Organizing Officer for a major labor union of South African garment industry workers, circa 1940
documents.content:
“I was born and raised on a farm, but my parents struggled to earn enough to support their
family. So, by the time I turned 18 [in 1930], I had to abandon ideas of further studies and
instead travelled to Johannesburg, to earn my own living in the clothing factories. How proud
I was on receiving my first week’s wages! I wrote home, telling my father that he now had
one mouth less to feed. I thought I would be able to save a sufficiently large sum to enable me
to continue my studies. But to my sorrow and disappointment, after only a few months, the
depression began to be felt in the clothing industry. Like many other workers, I was laid off.
What could I do now? I had no money to take me home and could expect no financial help from
my parents. So I stayed in the city, working a few hours here and there as I could, eating less
food, and living on practically nothing....
Nonetheless, I could not then, and cannot now agree with the arguments of those that say that
rural life is the basis of family life. I and thousands of my fellow working women in the factories
are moved by everyday financial problems and cannot become sentimental about the beauties
of the countryside. I am not afraid of the towns nor of the march of progress. I refuse to remain
a servant in the kitchen.”

documents.source: Ellen Kuzwayo, Black South African educator and women’s rights activist, describing the history of Johannesburg, from her autobiography published in 1985
documents.content:
“After the discovery of gold in 1886, Black men from different communities streamed into
Johannesburg which, at that time, was more or less a temporary mining camp. Men flowed
to the mines from the rural areas leaving their families behind to be cared for by the senior
woman in every home. Still, in those early decades most Black women lived on land which their
communities owned and which they cultivated.
But things changed for the worse in the [early decades of the twentieth century], when
communal land was removed from the control of Black people and communities were forced
to move from one area to another.1 So Black women were suddenly plunged into a situation of
accepting numerous roles of responsibility. Without warning or training, they became not only
mothers, but also family administrators, community counselors, and overall overseers of both
home and neighborhood.
For many women, the burden proved too much. Their response came in the form of moving
to cities themselves, often in search of their husbands or sons and despite stringent ‘influx’
regulations.2 As they were usually not allowed to live with their husbands in company
dormitories, they found a solution in domestic work, where they were provided with a room,
usually somewhere at the back of their White employers’ houses.”
land in the country that could be owned by Black farmers, effectively forcing most Black South Africans who lived
in rural areas to become tenant farmers on lands owned by Whites.
into “Whites-only” urban areas of the country

parts.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which the spread of industrialization provided women with new
opportunities and/or challenges during the period circa 1850 to 1950.
parts.scoring_notes: Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence from Documents (2), Evidence Beyond Documents (1), Sourcing (1), Complexity (1). Total: 7 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Responds to the prompt with a historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning about the extent to which the spread of industrialization provided women with new opportunities and/or challenges during the period circa 1850 to 1950. The thesis must either provide some indication of the reason for making that claim OR establish categories of the argument. Must consist of one or more sentences located in one place, either in the introduction or the conclusion. A thesis that merely restates or paraphrases the prompt does NOT earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Industrialization created significant new opportunities for women across Asia, Europe, and Africa — including wage labor, organizational roles, and access to public life — but these opportunities came alongside severe exploitation of women's labor, continued male oversight, and, for poor women especially, worse living and working conditions than before. The extent of benefit or harm depended heavily on class and region.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: establishes a historically defensible claim that evaluates EXTENT (both new opportunities and challenges) and sets up analytical categories (class, region) as a line of reasoning.

sample_responses.response: Although industrialization provided some women with wage income and organizational experience, it primarily increased their exploitation through dangerous factory conditions, low pay, and continued subordination to male managers, supervisors, and family authority — making industrialization a net challenge rather than a net opportunity for most working women of this period.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: makes a defensible evaluative claim about extent (challenges outweigh opportunities), establishes a line of reasoning about why (exploitation + continued subordination) that structures an argument.

sample_responses.response: Industrialization affected women in many ways during the period circa 1850 to 1950.
sample_responses.commentary: Does NOT earn the point: restates/paraphrases the prompt topic without establishing a defensible claim or any line of reasoning about extent.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Describe a broader historical context relevant to the prompt.

required_elements: Describe a broader historical context relevant to the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Describe a broader historical context relevant to the prompt.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Accurately describe a context relevant to the spread of industrialization and women's roles during the period circa 1850 to 1950. Must be more than a phrase or reference — must be a developed description of broader historical events, developments, or processes that occurred before, during, or continue after the time frame of the prompt and must relate these broader contexts to the argument. Merely mentioning a broader context does not earn the point. Acceptable contexts include: the Industrial Revolution originating in Britain and spreading globally; the rise of capitalism and factory-based production replacing cottage industries; imperialism and the integration of Africa, Asia, and Latin America into the global economy; early feminist and labor reform movements; the growth of colonial labor systems.

sample_responses.response: Beginning in Britain in the late 18th century and spreading globally through the 19th century, industrialization transformed economies from agricultural and artisan production to factory-based wage labor. This transformation drew millions of people — including large numbers of women — into urban manufacturing centers, fundamentally changing not only how goods were produced but also how families were organized, how gender roles were defined, and how class stratified access to economic opportunity.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: provides a substantive description of the broader historical context (global spread of industrialization and its transformation of economic and social organization) that is connected to the prompt's focus on women's opportunities and challenges.

sample_responses.response: European imperial expansion in the 19th and early 20th centuries integrated global regions into capitalist economic networks centered on extraction and export manufacturing. This imperial context meant that industrialization in places like South Africa, the Ottoman Empire, and Japan did not simply replicate the British model — it was shaped by racial hierarchies, colonial labor coercion, and unequal power relations that determined which women could access factory work and on what terms.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: accurately describes a broader historical context (imperial integration of global regions into capitalist networks) and connects it to the prompt topic by explaining how that context shaped women's industrial experience.
Does NOT earn the point: too vague and undeveloped — no substantive description of the broader context or connection to the argument about women's opportunities and challenges.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Docs: Use content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: Use content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Use content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Accurately describes — rather than simply quotes — the content from at least three of the documents to address the topic of industrialization and women's new opportunities and/or challenges circa 1850–1950. A response that merely quotes or paraphrases without describing the document's relevance does NOT earn the point. Must use at least three documents.

sample_responses.response: Document 1 shows that Russian women workers organized collectively through factory branch societies in the 1890s, with illiterate women like Maria Soldatova rising to leadership positions — demonstrating that industrialization created new opportunities for working-class women to participate in labor organizations. Document 3 describes Shanghai factory women working from morning into the evening under exploitative conditions in which capitalist factory owners extracted profit from their labor. Document 5 shows a Japanese girl deceived into factory work with false promises of easy work and good pay, then trapped in conditions of near-indentured servitude with wages consumed by deductions for food and supplies.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: accurately describes the content of three documents (Docs 1, 3, 5) and connects each description to the prompt topic (industrialization's opportunities and challenges for women).

sample_responses.response: Document 1 says 'I began the mass organization of women in the Factory Workers' Society.' Document 3 says 'Everywhere in Shanghai there are silk factories.' Document 5 says the work 'turned out to be hell.'
sample_responses.commentary: Does NOT earn the point: merely quotes documents without describing their content or explaining relevance to the topic.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Docs+: Support an argument using at least four documents.

required_elements: Support an argument using at least four documents.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Support an argument using at least four documents.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Support an argument in response to the prompt by accurately using the content of at least four documents. The four documents do not have to be used in support of a single argument — they can be used in support of opposing arguments or in a nuanced argument about the topic. Must use at least four documents to earn this point (in addition to the three needed for point a3).

sample_responses.response: The seven documents collectively support the argument that industrialization created both new opportunities and new challenges for women, but that the balance between these differed by region, class, and race. Documents 1 and 4 show opportunities: Doc 1 describes Russian women organizing labor societies and taking on leadership roles; Doc 4 shows that in rural Russia, women took over traditionally male roles as household heads and village council members when men left for factory work. Documents 3, 5, and 6 document severe challenges: Doc 3 shows Chinese women exploited by Shanghai factory capitalists; Doc 5 documents deceptive recruitment and near-indentured conditions for Japanese textile workers; Doc 6 shows a White South African woman laid off during economic depression with no safety net. Document 7 adds a racial dimension: Black South African women were forced into domestic service in White employers' homes, confined by apartheid influx regulations to the most constrained form of industrial-era work.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: uses content of all seven documents to explicitly support an argument about extent (both opportunities and challenges, differentiated by region/class/race).

sample_responses.response: Document 1 discusses women organizing. Document 3 discusses factory exploitation. Document 5 discusses deceptive recruitment. Document 7 discusses Black South African women.
sample_responses.commentary: Does NOT earn the point: names four documents but does not support an argument — no claim being made, no analytical connection between document content and a position on the prompt.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Beyond: Use at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence beyond the documents.

required_elements: Use at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence beyond the documents.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Use at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence beyond the documents.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Must use at least one specific piece of historical evidence relevant to industrialization and women's opportunities and/or challenges during the period circa 1850 to 1950 that is not found in the provided documents. The evidence must be specific and accurate — a general statement without named specifics does not earn the point. Acceptable examples include: British Factory Acts regulating women's and children's labor; the Triangle Shirtwaist Fire (1911) and its reform aftermath; the suffragette movement's connection to labor organizing; women's work in WWI industries; specific mill towns or factory districts; Japanese Meiji industrialization's use of female textile labor; the Lancashire cotton industry.

sample_responses.response: The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire in New York City in 1911, which killed 146 garment workers — mostly young immigrant women — demonstrated both that industrialization had created a new class of female industrial workers and that these workers faced life-threatening exploitation with few legal protections. The fire directly led to new factory safety regulations, illustrating how women's industrial experience also shaped reform movements.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: specific, accurate historical evidence (Triangle Shirtwaist Fire, 1911, specific details) that goes beyond the documents and is relevant to the topic of women's challenges in industrial work.

sample_responses.response: During World War I, many women entered industrial work for the first time as men left for the front lines — in Britain, France, and the United States, women took over positions in munitions factories, steel mills, and transportation that had previously been exclusively male, creating new industrial roles that some women leveraged into arguments for expanded political rights.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: specific historical evidence beyond the documents (WWI women's industrial labor in Britain, France, and the US, connection to suffrage arguments) relevant to both the opportunity and challenge aspects of the prompt.

sample_responses.response: Women worked in factories and faced many challenges.
sample_responses.commentary: Does NOT earn the point: too general — no specific evidence beyond the documents. Does not name a specific event, place, policy, or development.

scoring_points.description: Sourcing: Explain how/why the point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument.

required_elements: Explain how/why the point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Explain how/why the point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Must explain how or why — rather than simply identifying — the document's point of view (POV), purpose, historical situation, or audience is relevant to an argument that addresses the prompt for each of at least two documents. Merely identifying the author's background or document type does not earn the point — must explain how/why that sourcing element affects the document's usefulness, reliability, or meaning for the argument about industrialization and women. Must do this for at least two separate documents.

sample_responses.response: Document 3 is written by He-Yin Zhen, a Chinese political exile living in Japan who was affiliated with anarchist and feminist movements. Her purpose in writing was not merely to describe women's factory conditions but to use those conditions as evidence for a political argument about capitalism and class exploitation — meaning her account emphasizes suffering and exploitation rather than agency or opportunity. This purpose means we should use Doc 3 as evidence of the capitalist exploitation argument she was advancing, while recognizing she was unlikely to acknowledge any benefits industrialization might provide to working women.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: explains how He-Yin Zhen's PURPOSE (political writing for a radical activist audience) shapes the document's content and what argument it best supports — goes well beyond simply identifying her background.

sample_responses.response: Document 6's author, Johanna Cornelius, wrote an autobiographical statement prepared as a press release when she was elected as a union organizing officer — meaning her purpose was to present herself as a legitimate labor leader to a public and press audience. This public, promotional purpose means she frames her hardship narrative (farm to factory, depression-era layoffs) as a story of perseverance and labor solidarity rather than helplessness, potentially understating the structural disadvantages she faced to present herself as an effective leader.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: explains how the AUDIENCE and PURPOSE of the press release context shapes Cornelius's narrative choices — why she would frame her story in the particular way she does, which affects how we use the document as evidence.

sample_responses.response: Document 1 was written by Vera Karelina, a Russian woman. She was a labor organizer.
sample_responses.commentary: Does NOT earn the point: merely identifies the author's identity without explaining how/why her point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience is RELEVANT TO AN ARGUMENT about industrialization and women.

scoring_points.description: Complexity: Demonstrate a complex understanding through sophisticated argumentation and/or effective use of evidence.

required_elements: Demonstrate a complex understanding through sophisticated argumentation and/or effective use of evidence.
alternatives.correct_example: A response that successfully demonstrates: Demonstrate a complex understanding through sophisticated argumentation and/or effective use of evidence.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Demonstrates a complex understanding of the historical development that is the focus of the prompt through sophisticated argumentation and/or effective use of evidence. May demonstrate complexity through: explaining multiple themes or perspectives to explore nuance in the argument; explaining both similarity AND difference, or both continuity AND change, or both cause AND effect; explaining multiple causes or multiple effects; explaining relevant connections across time periods, geographical areas, or themes; qualifying or modifying the argument by considering diverse or alternative perspectives or evidence. The response must be developed and sustained throughout the essay — a single, isolated comment does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The documents reveal a structural complexity that a simple opportunities-vs.-challenges binary misses: the same industrialization processes that appeared to empower some women (Karelina's factory labor organizing, Doc 4's rural Russian women taking on village leadership roles) simultaneously created the material conditions for their deepest exploitation (Takai's near-slavery in Japanese textile factories, He-Yin Zhen's description of Shanghai's factory women). Furthermore, the global spread of industrialization interacted with existing racial hierarchies — as Doc 7 shows, for Black South African women industrialization produced not factory opportunities but domestic servitude confined to White employers' back rooms, a form of labor entirely excluded from the labor organizing and public participation documented for women elsewhere. This comparative dimension reveals that industrialization's effects on women cannot be assessed without accounting for the racial and colonial order through which it spread, complicating the standard narrative of industrial 'opportunity' as inherently progressive.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: demonstrates complexity through comparison across regions AND through qualification of the standard narrative — shows how race and colonialism produced fundamentally different experiences of industrialization, complicating any single-direction argument.

sample_responses.response: Industrialization had both good and bad effects on women.
sample_responses.commentary: Does NOT earn the point: acknowledges both opportunities and challenges but does not demonstrate complex understanding — no development of the nuance, no comparative analysis, no causal chain, no qualifying perspective.
